2022 · JMLR (arXiv) · Foundation Models

PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways

Google trained a 540-billion-parameter dense Transformer across two TPU v4 pods to settle a 2022 question: had language-model scaling plateaued? It hadn't. Few-shot accuracy kept climbing, a quarter of BIG-bench tasks jumped sharply at the top end, and chain-of-thought reasoning let the 540B model match finetuned state of the art.

Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts, Paul Barham, Hyung Won Chung, Charles Sutton, Sebastian Gehrmann, Parker Schuh, Kensen Shi, Sasha Tsvyashchenko, Joshua Maynez, Abhishek Rao, Parker Barnes, Yi Tay, Noam Shazeer, Vinodkumar Prabhakaran, Emily Reif, Nan Du, Ben Hutchinson, Reiner Pope, James Bradbury, Jacob Austin, Michael Isard, Guy Gur-Ari, Pengcheng Yin, Toju Duke, Anselm Levskaya, Sanjay Ghemawat, Sunipa Dev, Henryk Michalewski, Xavier Garcia, Vedant Misra, Kevin Robinson, Liam Fedus, Denny Zhou, Daphne Ippolito, David Luan, Hyeontaek Lim, Barret Zoph, Alexander Spiridonov, Ryan Sepassi, David Dohan, Shivani Agrawal, Mark Omernick, Andrew M. Dai, Thanumalayan Sankaranarayana Pillai, Marie Pellat, Aitor Lewkowycz, Erica Moreira, Rewon Child, Oleksandr Polozov, Katherine Lee, Zongwei Zhou, Xuezhi Wang, Brennan Saeta, Mark Diaz, Orhan Firat, Michele Catasta, Jason Wei, Kathy Meier-Hellstern, Douglas Eck, Jeff Dean, Slav Petrov, Noah Fiedel · Google Research
venue JMLR (arXiv 2204.02311) citations 8,211 topic Foundation Models source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By early 2022 the dominant NLP recipe still ran through finetuning. Encoder models like BERT and encoder-decoders like T5 were pre-trained with masked or span-corruption objectives and then adapted to each task with a labelled dataset and a round of gradient updates — meaning task-specific data collection and a separate set of weights to deploy per task. GPT-3 had shown a different path: a large decoder-only autoregressive model can be steered few-shot, from a task description and a handful of exemplars in the prompt, with no parameter updates at all.

Since GPT-3, a wave of models — GLaM, Gopher, Chinchilla, Megatron–Turing NLG, LaMDA — had pushed few-shot SOTA further, mostly by some mix of (1) more parameters, (2) more training tokens, (3) cleaner and more diverse data, and (4) sparsity. That raised two open questions PaLM set out to answer. First, the science question: does dense scaling keep paying off, or have returns flattened? Second, the systems question: nobody had trained a dense model anywhere near 540B parameters efficiently — prior efforts maxed out at ~4096 TPU v3 chips, confined to a single system or stitched together with pipeline parallelism. PaLM's bet was that you could push a single dense model to 540B parameters on 780B tokens, train it pipeline-free across two TPU v4 pods, and watch whether capability — including qualitatively new behaviour — was still emerging at that scale.

// how it works

The method

PaLM is a standard decoder-only Transformer trained with the ordinary left-to-right next-token objective — the architectural story is in the details that make a 540B dense model train well and decode cheaply. The MLP blocks use SwiGLU activations (three matmuls instead of two, but better quality per FLOP). Each block runs a "parallel" formulation: rather than feeding attention's output into the MLP, the two are computed from the same layer-normed input and summed, which fuses their input projections and runs roughly 15% faster at scale.

y = x + MLP(LayerNorm(x)) + Attention(LayerNorm(x)) // parallel layers — vs. the serial y = x + MLP(LayerNorm(x + Attention(LayerNorm(x))))

It also uses multi-query attention (key/value projections shared across heads, which barely moves quality but makes autoregressive decoding far cheaper on accelerators), RoPE rotary position embeddings, shared input/output embeddings, and no biases anywhere — the last for training stability at scale. The tokenizer is a lossless 256k-token SentencePiece vocabulary that splits numbers into individual digits and falls back to raw UTF-8 bytes, chosen to support a multilingual, code-heavy corpus. Three sizes share data and vocabulary: 8B (32 layers), 62B (64 layers), and 540B, so scaling can be read off three points.

Pathways: training across two pods

The headline systems result is that PaLM 540B was trained pipeline-free on 6,144 TPU v4 chips spanning two pods, using the Pathways system for two-way data parallelism at the pod level (gradients computed within each pod, then exchanged across the datacenter network and applied to keep both pods bitwise-identical). That reached 46.2% model-FLOPs utilization (and 57.8% hardware-FLOPs utilization) — a level of efficiency that hadn't been achieved for a dense model of this size, where the prior ceiling was 4,096 TPU v3 chips. Training on 780B tokens hit periodic loss spikes; the fix was pragmatic — rewind to a checkpoint ~100 steps before the spike and skip the offending data batches.

// what they found

The results

The throughline is that scaling had not plateaued. On the most widely used English benchmarks, few-shot PaLM 540B set a new state of the art on 28 of 29, measured against the best per-task result from GLaM, GPT-3, Megatron–Turing NLG, Gopher, Chinchilla, and LaMDA. On MMLU (5-shot), PaLM 540B averaged 69.3, edging the prior SOTA, Chinchilla 70B, at 67.5 — winning Humanities (77.0 vs 63.6), STEM (55.6 vs 54.9), and Social Sciences (81.0 vs 79.3) while trailing on "Other" (69.6 vs 73.9). The 8B and 62B models scored 25.3 and 53.7, so most of the gain is concentrated at the top end.

On BIG-bench — a then-new suite of 150+ deliberately hard tasks — PaLM 540B's 5-shot aggregate (a "normalized preferred metric") exceeded the average score of the human raters who attempted the same tasks, and it beat the prior SOTA on 44 of 58 tasks evaluated across all three model sizes. Roughly 25% of tasks showed "discontinuous" improvement — a sharp jump from 62B→540B rather than the smooth power-law gain seen elsewhere — the observation that fed the "emergent abilities" narrative. Note the bar, though: it's an aggregate, the model clears average human but stays far below best human.

The most influential result was on reasoning. Combining scale with chain-of-thought prompting, PaLM 540B reached 58% on GSM8K (8-shot CoT plus a post-hoc external calculator), beating the prior SOTA of 55% — which Cobbe et al. had obtained by finetuning a 175B model and training a separate verifier. The calculator alone never added more than 5%; the lift came from CoT plus scale. Across a suite of arithmetic and commonsense tasks, that combination matched or beat the finetuned state of the art, and on several (MAWPS, SVAMP) sailed past it — though it stayed below prior work on CommonsenseQA.

Code, multilingual, and memorization

Despite seeing only 39B code tokens in training (2.7B of them Python), PaLM 540B reached 26.2 pass@1 on HumanEval and 36.8 on MBPP — competitive with the code-specialist Codex 12B (28.8 on HumanEval) on roughly 50× fewer Python tokens. Finetuned into PaLM-Coder 540B, it hit 36.0 on HumanEval (matching Davinci Codex) and 47.0 on MBPP. Multilingual coverage was real but secondary — only ~22% of the corpus was non-English, yet few-shot translation matched or beat prior SOTA on several pairs. The paper also studied memorization honestly: the 540B model exactly reproduced the 50-token continuation for 2.4% of training examples versus the 8B model's 1.6% — bigger models memorize more, and memorization rises steeply with how often a string was duplicated in training.

Benchmark (setting)Prior SOTAPaLM 540B
MMLU avg (5-shot)67.569.3
BIG-bench, tasks beaten (of 58)—44
GSM8K (8-shot CoT + calc.)5558
HumanEval pass@1 (code)28.8 Codex 12B26.2
HumanEval pass@1 (PaLM-Coder)36.0 Davinci Codex36.0
// does it hold up?

The eval read

PaLM's evaluation is unusually thorough for 2022 — hundreds of benchmarks, an explicit train/eval overlap analysis, and dedicated bias, toxicity, and memorization studies — and the comparisons are against current, strong systems (GLaM, Gopher, Chinchilla, Megatron–Turing, LaMDA). Two things deserve a harder look. First, the comparison framing flatters PaLM: it pits PaLM's few-shot numbers against the best per-task result drawn from a basket of other models, so "SOTA on 28 of 29" is a strong-but-curated claim, not a head-to-head sweep.

Second — and this is the result history judged most sharply — PaLM is far from compute-optimal. It trains 540B parameters on 780B tokens (~1.4 tokens per parameter), where Chinchilla, published the same month, argued the optimum is closer to ~20. The tell is in PaLM's own numbers: a 70B Chinchilla came within two points of PaLM 540B on MMLU average (67.5 vs 69.3) with roughly eight times fewer parameters. PaLM is the better model, but it is also the more wasteful one — and the field promptly pivoted toward token-rich training (Llama, which cites PaLM, is the visible inflection).

baselines

Current and credible for 2022 (Chinchilla, Gopher, GLaM, Megatron–Turing, LaMDA), but reported as "best per-task across models," which inflates the SOTA-count headline relative to any single-model comparison.

contamination

Addressed better than most contemporaries: an explicit n-gram overlap analysis between training and eval sets, results on clean subsets, and a candid memorization study. Still self-reported, and the dataset/weights are closed, so none of it is independently checkable.

what's missed

"Beats average human on BIG-bench" is an aggregate over a curated subset against ~10 crowdworkers per task — PaLM stays far below best human. The "discontinuous improvement" framing was later contested as partly a metric artifact (Schaeffer et al.'s "emergent mirage," which cites this paper). GSM8K's 58% leans on a hand-tuned CoT prompt and an external calculator.

would raise confidence

A Chinchilla-optimal token-budget ablation at fixed compute; per-task BIG-bench breakdowns against best-human; reported variance across seeds/prompts; and — the big one — open weights and an open eval harness so the headline numbers are reproducible.

// bottom line

PaLM is the high-water mark of the "just scale the dense model" era and a genuine systems achievement — it proved capability was still climbing at 540B and that CoT+scale could rival finetuned reasoning pipelines. But it landed the same month as Chinchilla, which showed PaLM was badly under-trained for its size. The capability claims hold; the efficiency claim is where the paper aged, and the field's next two years were largely a correction to it.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Dense scaling hadn't plateaued. Few-shot PaLM 540B set SOTA on 28/29 English benchmarks and edged Chinchilla on MMLU (69.3 vs 67.5) — the gains concentrated at the largest scale.
  2. Chain-of-thought + scale rivaled finetuning. 8-shot CoT pushed GSM8K to 58%, beating a finetuned-175B-plus-verifier pipeline (55%) and helping cement CoT as the default reasoning prompt.
  3. Pathways was the enabling systems result. Training a dense 540B model pipeline-free across two TPU v4 pods (6,144 chips) at 46.2% MFU was as much an infrastructure milestone as a modeling one.
  4. It fed the "emergence" debate. ~25% of BIG-bench tasks jumped discontinuously from 62B→540B — evidence later reframed (the "emergent mirage" rebuttal) as partly a choice of metric.
  5. Chinchilla won the efficiency argument. At ~1.4 tokens/parameter PaLM was far from compute-optimal; a far smaller 70B Chinchilla came within two points on MMLU average, and token-rich training became the new default.
